title: Comparative Analysis of Conventional and Toroidal Propeller through CFD Methods
authors: Bima Anugerah Putra, Fajri Ashfi Rayhan, Muhamad Sugeri
abstract: Toroidal propeller comprising circular blades is gaining significant attention due to higher efficiency; lower noise levels and other advantages compared to the current propeller models. However, scientific publications on toroidal propeller is still limited, as the majority rely only on field experiments. Therefore, this study aimed to compare the performance of toroidal and conventional propeller to determine the improvement produced by toroidal propeller. The geometric parameters used to model the conventional propeller to resemble the toroidal included propeller diameter, number of leaves, hub diameter and blade front view. The pitch geometric parameter on the conventional propeller was varied to determine whether its efficiency would be similar to the toroidal propeller. In this study, each propeller model was simulated at 1000 - 6000 RPM using Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulation with Simscale software. This CFD simulation produced thrust and torque values which were calculated to obtain efficiency values. The results showed that thrust and torque values on the propeller increased along with high pitch and RPM. In terms of efficiency, a variation of the conventional propeller with a pitch of 171.45 mm produced higher efficiency values compared to the toroidal propeller, but had low thrust. Furthermore, visualization of fluid flow on each model showed that the best flow was shown by toroidal propeller without vorticity. These results provided valuable information on toroidal propeller and contributed to the further development of the technology design.
primary_domain: 流体力学 / 其他

title: The Study Quantifies Air Content in a Siphonic Roof Outlet (SRO) during Priming using CFD
authors: Akhmad Zikri, Ojo Kurdi, Syaiful Syaiful, Rusnaldy Rusnaldy, Mochamad Fahri Fadlu Robbi
abstract: The siphonic rainwater system is an effective drainage solution designed to handle the increased frequency of extreme rainfall caused by climate change. The Siphonic Roof Outlet (SRO) is a key component of this system, preventing vortex formation and air ingress while rapidly expelling air (priming time), thereby achieving full-bore flow. Studies measuring air content levels in SROs are still rare and primarily limited to experiments constrained by instrumentation accuracy. This study explores an approach to quantifying the air content level inside the SRO and examines its relationship with the flow field during the priming stage using Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD). The SRO features a circular concave baffle plate, a non-flat base plate, fins and two-way flow inlets. A full-scale experimental facility and a 3D numerical CFD model were developed, incorporating the RANS standard k-ε turbulence, the multiphase VOF, transient flow analysis and a water volume fraction α = 0.5 to represent the air-water interface. The findings revealed six operational zones, grouped into two regimes. The air content level fluctuates. In Regime 1, it drops rapidly and significantly by up to 46% within one second, with the flow behaving like a gravity-driven system. In Regime 2, the air content decreases progressively and fluctuates, influenced alternately by the flow rate or the suction effect of the SRO. The flow field in this regime is characterized by a complex, unsteady two-phase flow. The time required to reach a target flow rate of 26 l/s with a stable water level is 47 seconds. The circular-concave baffle plate causes vortices, turbulence and air pockets, negatively affecting the SRO's efficiency, prolonging priming times and creating disturbing noise. The noise impact of SROs in noise-sensitive buildings can be mitigated through measures such as noise insulation. Additionally, noise performance and priming time can be enhanced by streamlining the design of the baffle plate's lower section while simultaneously reducing the SRO's volume.

title: Evaluating the Tsunami Resilience of Residential Roof Types using CFD Modelling
authors: Chek Moon Wei, Chai Lee Jin, Hussein Adel Ali Mohammed, Noor Farahain Muhammad Amin, Yenn Teo Fang, Harish Selvam
abstract: Tsunami, among the most dreaded natural calamities, have recently struck various regions across the globe, causing substantial property damage and significant loss of life. Despite being rare, these events have prompted extensive research into the interactions between waves and buildings, particularly following the devastating 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami. However, the influence of different roof types on load responses has received limited attention, with prior studies primarily focusing on box-shaped buildings with flat roofs for simplicity. This study addresses this knowledge gap through numerical simulations investigating how various roof types affect the tsunami flow mechanism and the loads exerted on buildings. Typical single-story residential buildings with hip and curved roofs were subjected to a 3.5-m tsunami wave. The results demonstrated the significant role of roof shape in wave reflection and overtopping processes. Specifically, buildings with a curved roof design experienced a 13% greater wave impact on their front side due to wave reflection near the front of the building. The hip roof, on the other hand, allowed for wave overtopping, resulting in a 131% increase in loads on the building’s back face. These findings contribute to disaster risk reduction efforts by improving the understanding of the resilience of different residential roof types in vulnerable coastal communities.
primary_domain: 流体力学 / 其他

title: Quantifying Air Content in a Siphonic Roof Outlet During Priming using CFD: A Case Study of the NOFLAF-Type SRO
authors: Akhmad Zikri, Ojo Kurdi, Syaiful Syaiful, Rusnaldy Rusnaldy, Mochamad Fahri Fadlu Robbi
abstract: The siphonic rainwater system is an effective drainage solution designed to handle the increased frequency of extreme rainfall caused by climate change. The Siphonic Roof Outlet (SRO) is a key component of this system, preventing vortex formation and air ingress while rapidly expelling air (priming time), thereby achieving full-bore flow. Studies measuring air content levels in SROs are still rare and primarily limited to experiments constrained by instrumentation accuracy. This study explores an approach to quantifying the air content level inside the SRO and examines its relationship with the flow field during the priming stage using Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD). The SRO features a circular flat baffle plate, a flat base plate, a finless and three-way flow inlets. A full-scale experimental facility and a 3D numerical CFD model were developed, incorporating the RANS standard k-ε turbulence, the multiphase VOF, transient flow analysis and a water volume fraction α = 0.5 to represent the air-water interface. The findings revealed six operational zones, grouped into two regimes. The air content level fluctuates. In Regime 1, it drops rapidly and significantly by up to 29% within one second, with the flow behaving like a gravity-driven system. In Regime 2, the air content decreases progressively and fluctuates, influenced alternately by the flow rate or the suction effect of the SRO. A complex, unsteady two-phase flow characterizes the flow field in this regime. The time required to reach a target flow rate of 26 l/s with a stable water level is 40 seconds. The SRO design, incorporating a circular-flat baffle plate and a flat base plate, has proven effective in significantly reducing hydraulic jumps and vortices beneath the baffle plate, resulting in a more stable and laminar flow within the SRO. Further research is necessary to quantitatively evaluate and mitigate the impact of the SRO’s design and geometry on noise levels.

title: Design of an Air Temperature Sensor Using CFD and Neural Network-Based Error Correction
authors: Jie Yang, Jiaye Zhou, Jiale Jiang, Qingquan Liu
abstract: Air temperature sensors often exhibit measurement inaccuracies due to solar radiation effects, leading to overestimations of actual air temperature by up to $1~^{\circ }$ C. To address this issue, a novel temperature sensor with enhanced radiation shielding and ventilation was developed. The proposed design incorporates an S-shaped flow diversion device, two silver-plated mirrored aluminum plates, two hemispherical flow diversion structures, and a sensor probe. Computational fluid dynamics (CFDs) simulations were conducted to evaluate radiation shielding efficiency and airflow performance. Radiation-induced errors were assessed under varying environmental conditions, and a neural network algorithm was implemented to develop a correction model. Experimental results showed that, prior to correction, the average radiation-induced errors for the new sensor, the 41 003 model, and the 43 502 model were 0.443°C, 0.533°C, and 0.317°C, respectively, with maximum errors of 0.996°C, 1.42°C, and 0.905°C. After correction, the average radiation-induced error of the new sensor decreased to 0.147°C, with a maximum of 0.485°C. The 41 003 and 43 502 models achieved reductions to 0.171°C (maximum: 0.598°C) and 0.118°C (maximum: 0.37°C), respectively. The new sensor demonstrated a mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.434°C, root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.351°C, and correlation coefficient ( ${r}$ ) of 0.855 in corrected radiation-induced errors compared to experimental data. In contrast, the 41 003 model exhibited an MAE of 0.604°C, RMSE of 0.471°C, and r of 0.832, while the 43 502 model achieved an MAE of 0.268°C, RMSE of 0.218°C, and r of 0.901. These findings highlight the effectiveness of the new sensor design and correction model in mitigating radiation-induced measurement errors, providing improved accuracy for atmospheric temperature sensing applications.
primary_domain: 流体力学 / 其他

title: Enhancing Power Generation in Duct-Augmented Wind Turbines: The Role of Back Pressure and Pressure Recovery using CFD and a Modified BEM Model
authors: Aditya Rachman, Buyung Kosasih, Edward Ngii, Rudi Purwo Wijayanto, Amiral Aziz, Taurista Perdana Syawitri
abstract: The power enhancement of duct-augmented wind turbines (DAWTs) is primarily attributed to the increased mass flow rate driven by low back pressure at the duct outlet and pressure recovery inside the duct. This study investigates the role of back pressure and pressure recovery in flow augmentation and power extraction efficiency, providing insights into how duct design influences turbine performance. To achieve this, we employ Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations and a modified Blade Element Momentum (BEM) model, incorporating back pressure and pressure recovery coefficients. This integrated approach enables a comprehensive analysis of the aerodynamic interactions within the duct-rotor system, offering deeper insight into the relationship between back pressure, pressure recovery, duct geometry and turbine performance. The study is conducted in two stages: an empty duct simulation to assess the baseline aerodynamic behaviour within the duct and a full duct-rotor simulation to evaluate the interaction between the duct and the turbine. Results confirm that lower back pressure and enhanced pressure recovery play a critical role in power augmentation, while duct geometry significantly influences the magnitude of these effects. The findings highlight the importance of duct shape in improving power augmentation. These insights have important implications for the design and optimization of DAWTs, particularly for distributed and off-grid renewable energy applications. By strategically reducing back pressure and increasing pressure recovery, DAWTs can achieve higher efficiency and improved energy capture, making them a viable solution for small-scale wind power generation.
primary_domain: 流体力学 / 其他

title: CFD Investigation on Aerodynamic Performance of Variable Pitch Blades Under Low Wind Speed Conditions
authors: Muhammad Ashraf Azlan, Fazila Mohd Zawawi, Kamarulafizam Ismail, Nur Safwati Mohd Nor, Zarhamdy Md Zain, Dendy Adanta, Dewi Puspita Sari, Ahmad Fudholi, Ridwan Arief Subekti
abstract: Renewable energy refers to energy sources that are naturally replenished and can be sustained over time. These include solar, wind, hydro, geothermal and biomass energy. Among these, wind energy has emerged as a key contributor to global energy demands due to its efficiency and decreasing cost. In the early stages of wind energy development, most wind turbines operated at a constant speed. However, there has been a recent increase in the installation of variable pitch-blade wind turbines in wind farms, with more manufacturers producing this type of turbine. The use of variable pitch blades offers significant advantages, particularly in regions with low wind speeds. Studying the aerodynamic performance of blades at various pitch angles is essential in initiating the design process of horizontal-axis wind turbines (HAWTs) to achieve optimal performance. This investigation focuses on the performance of variable pitch blades between 0° which is rigid blade until 25°, particularly under low wind speed conditions found in Malaysia. The purpose of this research is to study the aerodynamic behaviour of HAWT blades and determine the most efficient blade chord design. Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations using ANSYS Fluent are employed to evaluate blade performance across different pitch angles and wind speeds. The results reveal that varying the pitch angle significantly affects the flow behaviour and aerodynamic characteristics of the blades. Specific pitch angles show improved lift generation and reduced flow separation, which contribute to higher turbine efficiency under low wind speed conditions. In conclusion, the use of variable pitch blades in HAWTs can enhance aerodynamic performance and energy output in low-wind regions. The findings of this study provide valuable insights into blade design optimization, supporting the development of more efficient wind turbine systems.

title: Thermal Behaviour Analysis in Finned Cross-Flow Heat Exchangers using Simulation
authors: Ali Mohammed Hussien, Wadhah Hussein Al Doori
abstract: Finned cross-flow heat exchangers are important to HVAC systems because of their compact design and efficient thermal performance. However, traditional numerical models often apply fictitious boundary conditions that are significantly different from the actual physical conditions of the system. This shortcoming emphasizes the lack of a more reliable approach to modelling. Consequently, the aim of this research is to design a multiscale three-dimensional CFD model that can more accurately predict heat transfer for turbulent flows in finned heat exchangers while incorporating contact resistance consideration. The simulation was performed in ANSYS CFX, which solves the RANS equations using the SST k-ω model for turbulence under steady state. A Grid Convergence Index (GCI) analysis was used to determine mesh independence, and inlet air velocities between 2 and 6 m/s were tested at a constant wall heat flux of 1.5 kW/m². A parametric study analysed the effect of increasing different fin shapes, providing an increase in fin yield, heat exchange, thermal resistance, and pressure drop. Contact resistance, as shown in the results, comprises around 10% of the total thermal resistance. Moreover, the increased fin height of four millimetres also increased heat transfer improvement to 19%, albeit with an increase in pressure drop of 27%. While no direct validation was done experimentally, the model was validated against manufacturer data and showed an 11% deviation in total thermal resistance. These results suggest that the CFD framework is physically accurate and computationally efficient for simulating real-world operating conditions for finned heat exchangers.

title: Numerical Simulation of Water Table Fluctuations in 2D Domain using the Finite Difference Method
authors: Priyanka Katkar, Chhaya Lande, Prakash Jadhav
abstract: A study introduces a 2-dimensional groundwater flow model that can predict the variation in the water table due to recharge or depletion. A numerically rigorous framework, based on the theory of groundwater flow and the effect of various parameters, is employed in the model. Groundwater flow, represented by a partial differential equation, which is nonlinear in nature, often known as the Boussinesq equation, is solved by the finite difference method. In this study, a compact domain is considered whose all four sides are impermeable and receives a vertical recharge at the basin’s location. At the same time, water is been pumped from the defined wells. The combined effect of these two activities is studied and the results are simulated. Formation of a water mound under the basins and a cone of depression under the wells is observed. The effect of anisotropic medium and semi-permeable aquifer bed, often called as leaky base, is also studied. The effect of semi-permeability of the base is clearly seen in the graphs. Similarly, recharge effect is visible in the form of mounds under recharge basins.
primary_domain: 流体力学 / 其他

title: Flow and Heat Transfer of MHD Dusty Nanofluid over a Moving Plate with Constant Surface Temperature
authors: Euwing Low, Syahira Mansur
abstract: Nanofluid is a type of fluid that contains nanometre-sized particles, which known as nanoparticles. When nanofluid is mixed with dust particles that contain millimetre-sized or micrometre-sized particles, it turns into dusty nanofluid. The presence of impurities in the nanofluid affects the fluid flow, which reduces the efficiency, pressure and the temperature differential. This paper considers the flow and heat transfer characteristics of dusty nanofluid over a moving plate in the presence of magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) with constant surface temperature. Three types of nanoparticles namely copper oxide (CuO), aluminium oxide (Al2O3) and titanium oxide (TiO2) are considered. The governing partial differential equations are converted into a system of non-linear ordinary differential equations using similarity transformation, then the non-linear ordinary differential equations are solved using bvp4c program in MATLAB software. The effect of non-dimensional governing parameters such as velocity ratio parameter, magnetic field parameter, volume fraction of the nanoparticle, volume fraction of the dust particle, mass concentration of the dust particle, fluid particle interaction parameter for velocity and fluid particle interaction parameter for temperature for fluid and dust phases of CuO-water, Al2O3-water TiO2-water dusty nanofluids are discussed and presented through graphs. The skin friction coefficient and Nusselt number are discussed and presented in tabular form. The numerical results are compared with previous existing results for validation. It is found that nanoparticles act as good thermal conductivity as well as show significant effect on velocity of fluid and dust phase.

title: Numerical Study on Heat Transfer in Non-Newtonian Hybrid Nanofluid Flow in Porous Media with Electromagnetic Field
authors: Sulochana Chalavadi, Sultana Begum
abstract: The interplay between electromagnetic forces and non-Newtonian fluid dynamics presents a complex and intriguing field of study, with significant implications for both theoretical physics and practical applications. This study examines the key characteristics of Casson hybrid nanofluid flow over a stretching surface under the influence of electromagnetic forces. The boundary layer flow is mathematically modelled and a similarity transformation technique is employed to derive the governing equations. A nanofluid is formulated by dispersing zinc oxide (Zno) and multi-wall carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) into a sodium alginate base fluid. The impact of various fluid flow parameters on momentum, energy and nanoparticle concentration profiles is analysed. Findings indicate that the application of an electric field enhances momentum and thermal boundary layer thickness, while the Casson parameter suppresses momentum distribution.

title: Effect of Reynolds Number on Localized Temperature Distribution in an Array of Rectangular Fins
authors: Wadhah Hussein Al Doori, Ali Mohammed Hussien
abstract: This research seeks to evaluate the impact of Reynolds number on local temperature distributions in a rectangular fin array for forced convection heat transfer processes. Tests were carried out on various lengths of fins at air speeds of 1.0 m/s to 2 m/s yielding a range of Reynolds numbers between 20800 to 37900. It was found that the higher the Reynolds number, the higher the surface temperature of the fins relative to the coolant air, particularly within the vicinity of the heat sources. The study also showed that distributions were affected due to the location of measurements, which were found to be highest at the base of the fins and lowest at the tips. The results also verified the supposition that an increase in the cooling airflow rate increases the forced convection heat transfer, which, in turn, lowers the temperature difference across the fins and thus, enhances the thermal effectiveness of the system. These results are important from the standpoint of fin design optimization in postulated high-efficiency cooling systems and thus the performance of heat exchangers in industrial and electronic applications is greatly improved.

title: Mathematical Modelling of the Two-Dimensional Advection-Diffusion-Reaction Model in Ethanol Production
authors: Ahmad Izul Fakhruddin Azimi, Norazaliza Mohd Jamil, Ilyas Khan
abstract: Ethanol fermentation is one of the renewable energy sources that replace fossil fuels. The fermentation process is complex due to its intricate chemical interactions, mixing and dependence on various factors aimed at optimising ethanol production. A mathematical model serves as a cost-effective method for studying the behaviour of the fermentation process. This study aims to develop a two-dimensional model for the batch ethanol fermentation process by incorporating particle movement and fluid dynamics into the classical kinetic model known as the advection-diffusion-reaction model. Furthermore, this study seeks to investigate the effects of agitator speed and position within the fermentation tank on ethanol production. The governing equations resulting from this are partial differential equations (PDE) that can predict the spatial distribution of microbes, substrates and ethanol within the fermentation tank. These equations are solved numerically using the Finite Volume Method (FVM), implemented in Python software. The study identified an increase in the free surface vortex area at the upper boundary of the tank due to agitation and the rise in agitator speed, reducing the concentration of ethanol production. Furthermore, the investigation into the placement of the agitator indicates that positioning it at the bottom of the tank produces a homogeneous solution and enhances ethanol production. This study highlights the importance of optimising agitator speed and position to maximise ethanol production. Furthermore, it enhances the understanding of particle behaviour in ethanol production through fermentation, thereby contributing to the advancement of sustainable biofuel technologies.

title: Co-Firing Coal and Sugarcane Bagasse Biomass in Stoker Boiler using Computational Fluid Dynamic Analysis
authors: Tony Suryo Utomo, Muchammad Muchammad, Saeful Bakhri
abstract: Greenhouse gas emission reductions are continuously pursued to improve air quality, mitigate climate change and ensure a cleaner, more sustainable environment. Sugarcane bagasse biomass is a viable cofiring with coal in coal-fired power plants because it has lower ash, sulphur and nitrogen content. This present study uses numerical analysis based on the Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) method to investigate the cofiring of sugarcane bagasse biomass and coal in the stoker boiler. The main focus is analysing temperature, emission gas CO2 and SO2 with a mixture ratio of 0%-50% biomass. Results show that increasing the sugarcane bagasse proportion leads to a reduction in furnace temperature (up to 10.5%), CO₂ emissions (up to 11.4%) and SO₂ emissions (up to 36.7%) due to the lower carbon and sulphur content of bagasse. The study also observed a decrease in combustion efficiency from 66% to 52% with higher bagasse ratios. These findings demonstrate that cofiring sugarcane bagasse offers a viable strategy for emission reduction, with an optimal balance observed at a 10% bagasse mix ratio for maintaining combustion performance.

title: Aerodynamic Performance of Bat-Inspired Flexible Flapping Wings
authors: Shafiq Suhaimi, Solehuddin Shuib, Hamid Yusoff, Abdul Rauf Abdul Rasam, Wan Mazlina Wan Mohamed
abstract: One of the main issues when addressing flapping wing design is the issue of negative lift generating during the upstroke motion of the flapping wing. In nature, it can be overcome by changing the shape of the wing using a complex combination of motions that is difficult to be replicated for a mechanical man-made wing. The objective of this study is to study the effect of flexible flapping wing mechanisms on the aerodynamic performance of a flapping wing. This is done through a Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulation of a flexible wing mechanism design based on bat wings at different stiffness. In the simulation, 2-way Fluid Structure Interaction (FSI) method was used where a Finite Element Analysis (FEA) coupled with CFD solver. The wing shape is based on a generalized version of an outline of a bat wing and the four-stiffness tested: fixed, high, medium, low, and free pivot. The simulation was done under 1m/s flight speed at 2.5Hz flapping frequency and an angle of attack from 0° to 45°. The result of the simulation shows that the lift generation increases with increasing wing flexibility until a certain point where the wing becomes too flexible, the generated lift begins to decay. In terms of drag, the difference between the wing stiffness is small. Analysis from the streamline shows that increasing flexibility generates a weaker wing tip vortex during the upstroke motion due to the bending motion of the wing which generates lower negative lift. This study shows the relations between the effect of the wing motion on the generation of the wing tip vortex and subsequent lift generation and aerodynamic efficiency. Further research will investigate the potential mapping of the aerodynamic efficiency of bat-inspired wings through geospatial modelling and analytical methods.

title: Turbulent Flow Simulation over a Backward-Facing Step Modelled in COMSOL
authors: Wajdy Q. Hussein, Mahir Faris Abdullah, Isam Jabbar Ibrahim, Rozli Zulkifli
abstract: This study deals with the simulation of turbulent flow along a backward directed step at Re= 5100. The main focus is on partially averaged Navier-Stokes (PANS) approach, as it covers both, turbulent flow in a channel and recirculation. The backward directed step, is a useful mean for a turbulence model to be studied to determine the performance efficiency and a solution system for it. A benchmark model was adopted to study the turbulence modelling, concentrating on numerous factors including, channel and recirculating flow. This type of modelling is widely used in Automotive industry design. The model covers turbulent flow over back step geometry at roughly Re=5100 and solved using a turbulence of k-epsilon model. To help understanding the geometry effects on flow conditions around a vehicle, a two-dimensional duct is used, to simplify the modelling. Then a comparison is conducted between a standard and experimental k-ε turbulence models, via the meshing that gives the best resulting criteria, then the result was compared with experimental data provided. It is found, between those two models, that the experimental k-ε turbulence model as more accurate and appropriate. Multiphase flows, fundamental numerical methods and turbulence model, among others, were experimented with, as they are a common backward-facing step validation mean to understand the benchmark for their evaluation. These models are also used by Combustion engines and HVAC in industry for validation. As an appropriate model have been determined, the behaviour of a flow over the body stated in literatures is analysed and compared with that found practically. The work shows that results and relationship of slope angle does not directly correlate with drag co-efficient, although the flow condition can be modelled by simulation. Generally modelling is a very useful tool but experimental testing can never be replaced.

title: A Special Application of Coupled Heat and Mass Transfer Problem in the Freezing Process for Vietnamese Pangasius
authors: Thanh Tam Thi Nguyen, Phu Quang Pham
abstract: Food preservation technology plays a vital role in quality extension and shelf-life preservation. In the case of frozen fish products, the precise calculation of freezing parameters for the refrigeration systems can relieve the power consumption on the industrial scale. Due to the variation of thermophysical properties of water contained in food during the freezing process, this process becomes more complicated in the phase-change period when water releases the latent heat. Simulating the freezing problem, considering the combination of heat and mass transfer, is challenging for products with complex shapes such as pangasius fillets. In this study, the coupled heat and mass transfer modelling is firstly employed to describe thermophysical properties of a three-dimensional Pangasius fillet using the Finite Element procedure. The results showed that the time for the whole Pangasius fillet to reach a temperature of -18C was 2400 s with the freezing temperature of -30C and air velocity of 5 m s-1, and also described the variation of the temperature field and moisture content of Pangasius fillet during the freezing process. The numerical results of transient coupled problem are obtained from the output of the COMSOL Multiphysics solver to demonstrate the benefits of the proposed approach.

title: Analysis of the Effect of Time Step Variation on Velocity Profiles in Free Surface Water Channels
authors: Priyo Heru Adiwibowo, Triyogi Yuwono, Wawan Aries Widodo
abstract: Hydropower is one of the top renewable energy sources due to its continuous generation potential. Among its technologies, hydrokinetic energy utilizes river currents to generate power using water turbines. However, low flow velocities in hydrokinetic systems require specially designed turbine blades. Savonius hydrokinetic turbines have gained attention for their efficiency in such conditions. This study introduces a free-surface water channel model that operates with minimal pump discharge, alongside a closed-loop water channel commonly used in wind tunnel experiments to optimize turbine performance testing. The analysis employs Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations to compare steady-state and transient approaches in water channel simulations. Results indicate that steady-state velocity profiles exhibit significant fluctuations, whereas the transient approach provides more stable and consistent distributions. This suggests that transient simulations better capture flow dynamics and velocity variations over time. Across various positions, the transient approach consistently yields more reliable results, making it the preferred method for accurately analysing turbine performance and fluid behaviour around the blades.
primary_domain: 流体力学 / 其他

title: Analytical Approximation of Solitary Waves with Compact Support for Fractional Nonlinear Dispersive K(m,n) Equations
authors: Che Haziqah Che Hussin, Arif Mandangan, Mohammed Said Souid
abstract: The study of solitons and compactons plays a crucial role in nonlinear physics. In this paper, we introduce the Multistep Modified Reduced Differential Transform Method (MMRDTM), which integrates Adomian polynomials with a multistep approach. This novel technique efficiently generates analytical approximations in a rapidly converging sequence while requiring fewer computed terms. By modifying the Reduced Differential Transformation Method (RDTM) and incorporating Adomian polynomials to handle nonlinear terms, the MMRDTM simplifies the solution process for nonlinear initial value problems with reduced computational effort. Furthermore, the multistep approach extends the solution’s convergence over a wider time domain. To validate the effectiveness of MMRDTM, we apply the method in two examples of fractional nonlinear Korteweg–de Vries equations (fNKdVEs) with compacton solutions. Graphical representations illustrate the precision and reliability of the technique. The results confirm that the MMRDTM can achieve better approximations to exact solutions efficiently.
primary_domain: 流体力学 / 其他

title: Impact of Wave Directions on Connector Forces in Hexagonal Modular Floating Structure
authors: Nur Hanani Ahmad Azlan, Nik Mohd Ridzuan Shaharuddin, Arifah Ali, Farah Ellyza Hashim, Sunarsih Sunarsih
abstract: The preliminary design of a hexagonal modular floating structure (HMFS) system comprises three configurations: HP-shaped, HV-shaped, Linear-Tandem. Each configuration connects six hexagonal modules that form a network of connectors. The performance of these connectors, particularly their ability to withstand forces induce by wave motion, plays a critical role in determining the configuration of the connector network. This paper outlines the establishment of the HMFS connector network and examines the impact of connector horizontal force using the three types of configurations. Simulations were conducted for regular waves in the following directions: 0°,30°,45°,60°,85°, and 90° in order to propose design concepts for HMFS configurations. The analysis considers the influences of various wave directions and HMFS configurations on the connector forces. The analysis indicated that the connector force in the HP-shaped configuration is 28% higher at first parallel connector orientation and 89% higher at first vertical angle connector facing 00 wave direction compared to the load in Linear-Tandem configurations. The HP-shaped configuration, which has hexagonal sides oriented parallel to the wave direction, experiences greater connector force by 23% at 00 wave direction on connector C3 than the HV-shaped configuration, which aligns its hexagonal vertices with the wave direction. The determination of horizontal connector load of hexagonal modules with varying configurations enabled the designer to estimate the horizontal connector load for various conceptual designs of hexagonal shapes, such as fish cages, dock ships, yacht terminal and floating cities.

title: Numerical Study of the Effect of Deflector Length on the Water Channel Wall on the Performance of Savonius Water Turbine
authors: Heru Mirmanto, Achmad Adrio Athallasyah, Triyogi Yuwono
abstract: In various countries, research activities in the field of renewable energy have increased significantly, due to the global energy crisis. In Indonesia, particularly in rural areas, numerous water canals remain underutilised as potential energy sources. Savonius-type water turbines, with their simple shape, are certainly easy for residents in rural areas near water canals to make. However, its efficiency is the lowest among other types of turbines, so efforts are needed to increase its efficiency. This research is part of an effort to increase the efficiency of the Savonius water turbine by installing a pair of deflectors in front of it. The effect of the length of the deflector installed on the canal wall on the performance of the Savonius turbine is presented in this paper. The study was conducted numerically using the computational dynamics software Ansys-Fluent. The simulation results show that the longest deflector, L = 891 mm and L = 773 mm, corresponds to the deflector angle  = 5o and  = 15o and the middle deflector, L = 290 mm and L = 400, corresponds to the deflector angle  = 5o and  = 15o are not able to increase the turbine performance relative to the conventional turbine without a deflector. The shortest deflector, L = 106 mm and L = 231 mm, corresponds to the deflector angle  = 45o and  = 60o can finally enhance the turbine's performance. In this case, the maximum power coefficient of the turbine with a deflector can increase the performance of the Savonius turbine by 39% compared to the maximum power coefficient of the conventional Savonius turbine without a deflector. It appears that the flow friction on the deflector surface, which increases with increasing deflector length, is responsible for the reduction in Savonius turbine power.

title: Characteristics of Cattaneo-Christov Heat Flux of MHD Casson Nanofluid Involving Gyrotactic Microorganisms through a Stretched Non-Flatness Sheet and has Variable Thickness
authors: Hymavathi Dyapa, Yuvaraju Namala
abstract: The primary goal of this study is to examine the bio convection of the Cattano-Cristov heat flux model for Casson nanoparticles incorporating microorganisms on a non-flatness sheet. MHD Non-Newtonian Casson fluid involving Gyrotactic microorganisms’ fluid flow with variable thickness through a stretched non- flatness surface is investigated computationally. For this investigation thermo diffusion effects and model of Cattaneo- Christov thermal flux is enforced to forecast consequences of thermal relaxation duration. The boundary layer equations are normalised and appropriate boundary conditions are added by transforming the governing partial differential equations using similarity transformation. Bvp5c technique with Matlab solver is used to acquire the numerical results for the equation system. The behaviour of new parameters on temperature, velocity, concentration distribution and motile rate of gyrotactic microorganisms is discussed and graphically analysed. These parameters include the magnetic field parameter, power index, Casson fluid number, Prandtle number, variable viscosity parameter, Soret number, Schimidt number, Lewis number and Peclet number. For the range of the defined physical parameters A=1; B=0.1; m=0.1; M=0.5; Pr=0.7; Sc=0.5; Sr=0.5; K=0.5; C=0.1; E=0.1; Lb=0.1; Pe=0.5; D=0.1; F=0.1;an increased Lewis figure implies the concentration separation flow has lower penetration depth and a decreased Brownian diffusion coefficient. And concentration of nanofluid particles and density of motile organism’s profiles are decreasing, which allows us to determine how big of an influence this has on mass transfer.

title: Numerical Study on Vortex-Induced Vibration (VIV) Energy Harvesting of Multiple Cylinders in Different Configurations
authors: Fatin Alias, Mohd Afifi Jusoh, Mohd Hairil Mohd, Erwan Hafizi Kasiman, Mohd Asamudin A. Rahman
abstract: The growing energy crisis highlights the need to explore alternative renewable energy sources for efficient energy harvesting. One such method involves utilizing the Vortex-Induced Vibrations (VIV) phenomenon in order to generate hydrokinetic energy from ocean currents. The vibrations produced by VIV convert hydrokinetic energy into electrical energy. This study aims to investigate the effects of different cylinder arrangements on vibration amplitude and power conversion efficiency in a VIV energy converter model. Simulations were conducted with a mass ratio of 1.68 and a Reynolds number of 82,000, focusing on multiple cylinders in different configurations. Results indicate that the in-line configuration achieved the highest average power-to-volume density at 115.57 W/m³. This analysis provides valuable insights for optimizing VIV-based energy harvesting devices, enhancing their efficiency in extracting usable energy.

title: Effects of Passenger Capacity and Climate Control Settings on the Thermal Comfort Levels of Automated Rapid Transit (ART) System
authors: Aina Balqis Azrin, Shafiq Suhaimi, Hazran Husain, Wan Mazlina Wan Mohamed
abstract: Thermal comfort plays an important role in the quality of life of people especially for a hot and humid environment such as experience in Malaysia. This is issue is even more important for a small and tight conditions filled with crowded people like found in a public transportation setting. A well-designed climate control system will not only give comfort to the passengers of these public transportation system but also ensures their health and well-being. This study aims to investigate the effects of number of passengers and climate control settings on the thermal comfort of passengers in a new Automated Rapid Transit (ART) transit system. This is done by conducting a Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulation to simulate the heat dissipation and airflow inside of an ART carriage model. The inlet air velocity of the climate control system was set at blower level; level 2 (1.6 m/s), level 4 (2.9 m/s), and level 6 (3.2 m/s), the climate control temperature that were set at 14.7 °C and 8.7 °C, and the passenger capacity tested are; low capacity (16 passengers), nominal capacity (74 passengers), and maximum capacity (90 passengers). The thermal comfort of the passengers was measured by calculating the level of Predicted Mean Vote (PMV) and the Predicted Percentage Dissatisfied (PPD). According to ASHRAE Standard 55-2020, the permissible PPD level must fall within -0.5 to +0.5 for the climate to be considered comfortable. Form the results, it was found that the combination of level 4 air velocity and 8.7 °C thermal settings has is the optimal setting for nominal and maximum passenger capacity. However, there are no combinations that resulted in zero passenger discomfort since all results shows that there are passengers in the corners or standing in the centre that falls too high or too low on the comfort scale. This is due to the air outlet conditions and passenger seating layout. The results from this study shows the relations between passenger capacity and thermal comfort and the data collected can be used for carriage design for maximum thermal comfort.

title: Control of Base Flows using Cavities at Supersonic Mach Number
authors: Nik Deanna Asheera Nik Hishamuddin, Mir Sohail Ali, Ambareen Khan, Sher Afghan Khan, Khizar Ahmed Pathan, Ramchandra P. Chopade
abstract: The occurrence of sudden expansion and flow at the blunt base in the aerospace industry is widespread. Due to flow separation at the base region, a recirculatory region forms, where the pressure is lower than the ambient pressure, resulting in a considerable base drag value. This study investigates the regulation of base flows that are suddenly expanded at a Mach number of 1.6. This study uses internal flow methods to simulate the flow field in the recirculation zone using the passive control technique in the form of a cavity. This paper covers geometrical parameters, including an area ratio of 2.25 and a length-to-diameter ratio of 1:6. Cavities were used as passive controls, and aspect ratios of 3:3 and 6:3 were used to control the base pressure. The location of the cavities was changed from 0.5D to 2D. The inertia parameters considered are the nozzle pressure ratio and the Mach number M = 1.6. The cavity is a passive control method to regulate base pressure and drag. Based on the results, improvements can be made to the aerodynamic design to meet the design specifications. This work also provides the design process for a passive control in terms of the cavity for suddenly expanded flow in a nozzle with a supersonic flow Mach number. The cavity does not adversely influence the flow field in the duct.
primary_domain: 流体力学 / 其他

title: Concentrated Flow Effects on Aerodynamics Performance for CyFlaP Magnus UAV by using Computational Approach
authors: Hidayatullah Mohammad Ali, Azmin Shakrine Mohd Rafie, Mohd Faisal Abdul Hamid, Syaril Azrad Md Ali
abstract: The Cylinder-to-Flat Plate embedment (CyFlaP) Magnus Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) utilises a rotating cylinder propulsion mechanism that benefits from the Magnus effect to enhance aerodynamic performance. While previous studies have demonstrated that this design generates substantial lift, the presence of large flow separation bubbles atop the surface remains a critical limitation, leading to increased drag and reduced aerodynamic efficiency. To address this issue, this study investigates the effects of concentrated airflow on the aerodynamic characteristics of the CyFlaP by incorporating a fixed top plate above the model. By directing airflow over the rotating cylinders, the top plate is hypothesised to mitigate separation effects and enhance lift. This approach optimises the Magnus effect while reducing aerodynamic inefficiencies caused by flow separation bubbles. A computational fluid dynamics (CFD) analysis was conducted using ANSYS Workbench to evaluate aerodynamic performance under varying conditions. The study parameters included inlet wind speeds ranging from 5 m/s to 15 m/s, gap sizes between the top plate and the main structure varying from 5 mm to 30 mm and angles of attack from 0° to 20°. The k-omega Shear Stress Transport (k-ω SST) turbulence model was employed to capture flow separation and vortex formation accurately. The results indicate a significant enhancement in lift coefficient (C_L), with improvements of up to 120% compared to reference values. The study also reveals that smaller gap configurations between the top plate and the CyFlaP structure facilitate better concentrated airflow, effectively reducing separation bubbles and improving overall aerodynamic efficiency. Additionally, the modified design demonstrated a delay in stall onset at higher angles of attack, which is crucial for maintaining lift at increased wind speeds. However, the findings also suggest that while lift is improved, drag values increase at higher speeds, indicating the need for further design refinements, such as smoothing the leading edge of the top plate. In conclusion, the incorporation of a top plate in the CyFlaP Magnus UAV significantly enhances aerodynamic performance by optimising concentrated airflow. These findings establish a foundation for optimising top plate configurations in Magnus UAVs, offering a potential path to enhanced lift efficiency and reduced aerodynamic losses.

title: JutulDarcy.jl - a fully differentiable high-performance reservoir simulator based on automatic differentiation
authors: Olav Møyner
abstract: Abstract Reservoir simulators are highly complex computer programs and are often treated as “black boxes” that act on standardized input formats. In part, this is due to the closed-source nature of commercial offerings commonly used in industry, but even when the source-code is available, modification of a fully featured simulator can be a daunting task: A reservoir simulation problem, when fully specified, can have a great number of parameters and functional relationships that are tightly coupled together in most implementations. This is an obstacle to workflows that go beyond forecasting and into optimization, parameter fitting and sensitivity analysis where the solution quantity of interest should be paired with gradients taken with respect to forces and parameters. The monolithic design of typical reservoir simulation codes cannot be separated from the technical and mathematical choices made when discretizing the governing equations: The implicitness required to handle pressure changes and strong coupling between different solution variables means that the Jacobian of the governing equations must be implemented with respect to the primary variables: An error-prone process when done manually, and highly time-consuming when considering coupled systems. Automatic differentiation (AD) is a common cure to this problem that can generate Jacobians, but often comes at a severe cost in runtime performance. A more practical issue is that high computational performance for simulation problems relevant to modern geoenergy operations necessitates implementation in a compiled language while optimization and history matching workflows that integrate many data sources are most naturally expressed in high-level scripting languages. We describe the design of JutulDarcy, an open-source porous media simulator written in the Julia language. JutulDarcy is designed from the ground up with extensibility and computation of sensitivities in mind, starting from the assumption that high-performance automatic differentiation enables us to radically rethink the way simulators are designed. Highlights of this design include arbitrary execution graphs for the equation terms, in which dependence relationships can be re-configured on the fly, easy coupling of models, computation of sensitivities with respect to all model parameters through an adjoint method with a parameter declaration system, and an assembly process with performance that can exceed that of compiled simulators with hand-coded Jacobians. We validate the simulator on a number of standard test cases (immiscible, black-oil, CO2 and compositional flow) and demonstrate MPI parallel performance on multi-million cell models. In addition, we offer a frank assessment of the relative strengths and weaknesses of the Julia programming language for scientific computing.
primary_domain: 流体力学 / 其他

title: Effect of Airfoil Geometry on the Performance Small Scale Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine in Remote Laboratory Simulator
authors: Ahmad Fauzan Adziimaa, Ignatius Elang Suseno, I Putu Eka Widya Pratama, Natasya Tiara Regina
abstract: Coal mining and its utilization are significant contributors to greenhouse gas emissions and air pollution, adversely affecting climate change and human health, particularly in areas proximate to power generation facilities. The transition to renewable energy sources, such as wind power, is a crucial solution, especially in Eastern Indonesia, which possesses substantial wind potential. The design of wind turbine blades (airfoil) plays a key role in determining energy generation efficiency. This study evaluates the aerodynamic performance of several airfoil profiles such as SG6043, NACA44112, E231, S1210 and SD2030, selected for high lift-to-drag ratio values. The study involved Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations using ANSYS Fluent, employing a rotating reference frame and meticulous mesh generation. Based on simulation results, the airfoil profile yielding the highest torque was selected for blade manufacturing via 3D printing. Power coefficient (Cp) calculations revealed that SD2030 blades outperform SG6043 blades, achieving Cp values of 0.3764, 0.5298 and 0.6508, compared to the 0.1194, 0.4157 and 0.4776 for SG6043. Additionally, SD2030 blades demonstrated higher efficiencies of 22.31%, 31.4% and 39.83%, respectively, over the 11.5%, 24.64% and 28.3% achieved by SG6043 blades. These results indicate that SD2030 blades demonstrate superior efficacy in extracting wind kinetic energy. Furthermore, a developed remote monitoring system enables effective real-time tracking of turbine performance, thereby enhancing operational oversight.

title: Numerical Investigation of Hydrothermal Performance in Curved Plate-Fin Heat Sinks with Crosscuts under Forced Convection: A Comparative Analysis with Straight Plate-Fin Designs
authors: Muhammad Qasim Nawaz, Khurram Altaf, Ghulam Hussain
abstract: Increased performance and rapid miniaturization in the electronics industry demand high power dissipation in electronic systems. Therefore, researchers tend to investigate novel heat transfer enhancement methods to achieve the required cooling capacity. The present study performs a comparative hydrothermal performance analysis of innovative Curved Plate Fin Heat Sink (CPFHS) and Straight Plate Fin Heat Sink (SPFHS) with and without crosscut configurations under forced air-cooling conditions, considering SPFHS without any crosscuts as the reference model. The numerical study is conducted using ANSYS FLUENT CFD software, utilizing the k-ε turbulence model. Results indicate that the inclusion of crosscuts in CPFHS leads to a higher enhancement in the Nusselt number, compared to SPFHS. As compared with the SPFHS, Nusselt number enhancement of 16% and 6.8 %, pressure drop increment of 31.3% and 12% and hydrothermal performance factor (HTPF) enhancement of 6% and 3% are recorded for curved crosscut plate-fin heat sink with 8 crosscuts (CCPFHS-8) and straight crosscut plate-fin heat sink with 8 crosscuts (SCPFHS-8), respectively. Based on these findings, CCPFHS-8 is recommended as the optimum heat sink configuration, with a maximum reduction of 2.95K in the base temperature as compared to the SPFHS, out of all the configurations tested in this study. Application of CCPFHS-8 for cooling purposes may lead to the reduced failure rate of electronic components, due to reduction in component temperature.

title: Numerical Study of Heat Transfer on using Metal Foam and Nanofluid in Double Pipe Heat Exchanger
authors: Mohammed Baqer Nati, Abbas J. Jubear Al-Jassani, Mohammed Ghalib Al-Azawy, Adel G. Nasser
abstract: This study investigates the effect of adding metal foam and nanofluids on the performance of a double pipe heat exchanger. Water was used as a working fluid at flow rate 2,3,4,5 Lpm. The outer pipe has diameter 60 mm and length 609.6mm and an inner pipe with a diameter 20 mm. The numerical simulation was conducted by using ANSYS FLUENT 2022 R1 software. Metal foam with the porosity of 0.9 and 40 pores per inch (ppi) was used. The cases considered in the analysis including a heat exchanger with a complete fill of copper foam. The Results showed improvement in heat transfer rate when added metal foam with circle holes by 78% and it increased by 28% when using CuO with concertation 4% while, the amount of heat transfer rate increased by 184% when adding metal foam with circle holes combined with 4% CuO. The results also showed that metal foam with circle holes provide Performance evaluate criteria (PEC) of 1.92 and CuO with concertation 3% produce PEC of 1.3 while, metal foam with circle holes combined with 1% CuO provide the maximum value PEC of 2.14.

title: Heat Transfer Enhancement in an Intake Manifold for Internal Combustion Engines using Nanoparticles
authors: Hayder Kurji, Mokdad Hayawi Rahman, Najah Mahdi L. Al Maimuri, Emad Q. Hussein, Murtdha S. Imran, Farhan Lafta Rashid
abstract: The introduction of nanoparticles into thermal systems has enhanced heat transfer efficiency, offering significant benefits to the industrial and automotive sectors. Researchers evaluate the impact of Al₂O₃, CuO, SiO₂, ZnO and carbon-based nanoparticles on an internal combustion engine’s thermal performance on the intake manifold. The study investigates the effect of varying nanoparticle volume fractions, which vary between 0.01 and 0.1, the outlet air temperature, temperature drop, pressure drop, mechanical efficiency and heat transfer. The outcomes indicate that the addition of nanoparticles significantly enhances the heat transfer coefficient. Using ZnO and carbon-based nanofluids yields a 28% enhancement in heat transfer coefficient and a significant reduction in outlet air temperature. The mechanical efficiency enhancement falls within a 5% to 7% range. The findings establish key guidelines for selecting nanoparticles that enhance thermal management within engine intake manifolds, leading to improved engine performance with reduced emissions.

title: Grid Convergence Index for Aeroacoustic Investigation of Airfoil Leading Edge Noise Utilizing Delayed Detached Eddy Simulation and Acoustic Analogy
authors: Muhammad Imran Firdaus Kamardan, Mohamed Sukri Mat Ali, Nurshafinaz Maruai, Rafiansyah Putra
abstract: Turbulent impingement noise is a major contributor to airfoil self-noise near the leading edge. This particular airfoil noise is usually simulated through rod-airfoil configuration where the cylinder creates a turbulent wake that interacts with airfoil leading edge. As with any numerical simulation, a grid independence study is needed to ensure the accuracy of results without affecting the mesh number. In this study, three different mesh namely coarse, medium and fine were used in rod-airfoil configuration to investigate the grid independency of the simulation. A 10D gap distance between the rod-airfoil was used with 1D rod and 10D chord length of the NACA0012 airfoil. The geometrical case of the different mesh grids is then submitted to the cluster computer running in parallel consist of six nodes. The simulation was performed using open-source code OpenFOAM software with flow calculations using a hybrid method of Delayed Detached eddy simulation (DDES) coupled with the Spalart-Allmaras (SA) turbulence model. The aerodynamic noise calculation is then solved by using the Curle acoustic analogy for the far field acoustic pressure. Grid convergence index (GCI) and Richardson extrapolation were calculated from three different mesh results based on the coefficient of lift, drag and Strouhal number from the cylindrical rod. This study is required for the validation with the experiment. The results show monotonic convergence between the three mesh grids while the further finer mesh configuration does not significantly change the simulation results. Therefore, the fine mesh configuration can be utilized for further investigation of numerical simulation involving the incorporation of geometric design to reduce the turbulent impingement noise.

title: MHD Mixed Convective Chemically Reacted HNF (Fe3O4-SiO2/H2O) Flow Analysis over a Stretching Cylinder
authors: Chundru Maheswari, Bhavanam Naga Lakshmi, E. Gouthami, D. Vijaya Kumar, S. Venkata Subrahmanyam, Ravuri Mohana Ramana
abstract: Hybrid nanofluids demonstrate superior thermal conductivity, mechanical resilience, chemical stability and physical strength compared to conventional nanofluids. The cooling rates of hybrid nanofluids depend on their viscosity and the specific geometric configuration of the flow path. Cylindrical geometries are crucial in heat transfer due to their widespread applications across various industries. Our aim in this study is to examine the heat and mass transfer variations in a two-dimensional, steady-state, axisymmetric, viscous, laminar and incompressible MHD Fe3O4 - SiO2/H2O hybrid nanofluid flow, influenced by the parameters such as the mixed convection parameter (0 ≤ λ ≤ 15), concentration to thermal buoyancy forces ratio parameter (1 ≤ N ≤ 10), curvature parameter (0 ≤ γ ≤ 2), Hartmann number (0 ≤ Ha ≤ 15), Darcy number (1 ≤ Da ≤ 10), thermal radiation (1 ≤ Rd ≤ 7), heat source/sink (-1 ≤ Q ≤ 0.5), chemical reaction parameter (1 ≤ Rc ≤ 6), Eckert number (0 ≤ Ec ≤ 0.7) and Schmidt number (1 ≤ Sc ≤ 1.6) over a porous linearly stretchable cylinder. The Tiwari-Das model is implemented to investigate the flow behaviour. A key innovation of this research is the application of the prescribed heat flux stretchable permeable cylinder with thermal boundary constraint. Nonlinear PDEs generate the ODEs through the application of similarity transformations. The confluent hypergeometric function was used to solve the energy equation under the boundaries of prescribed heat flux. The numerical solutions are derived using BVP5C method in MATLAB, utilizing an approach of shooting technique. This study reveals that the velocity rises with progressive values of λ, N and γ, whereas an inverse trend is noticed with increasing Ha and Da. The temperature progresses, the hiking of λ, N, γ, Ha, Da, Rd, Ec and Q. The profile of Concentration enhancing in Ha, Da and γ hiking, while the trend is opposite for enhancing the λ, Sc and Rc. Skin friction declines by hiking of γ, Ha, Da and Rc. In contrast, it exhibits an opposite trend concerning the λ, N and Rd. The Nusselt number rises with increasing λ, N and Rd. Meanwhile, the Sherwood number tends to increase with higher values of the Ha and Da, but decreases with rising λ, N, γ, Rd and Rc. This model has been thoroughly validated with existing data and has shown outstanding accuracy. The results offer fresh perspectives on the complex interactions among different physical phenomena in hybrid nanofluid mixed convection flows.

title: Rapid Prediction of High-Resolution 3D Ship Airwake in the Glide Path Based on CFD, BP Neural Network, and DWL
authors: Qingsong Liu, Gang Ren, Dingfu Zhou, Bo Liu, Zida Li
abstract: To meet the requirements of the high spatiotemporal three-dimensional (3D) airflow field within the glide path corridor during carrier-based aircraft/unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) landings, this paper proposes a prediction method for high spatiotemporal resolution 3D ship airwake along the glide path by integrating computational fluid dynamics (CFD), backpropagation (BP) neural network, and Doppler wind lidar (DWL). Firstly, taking the conceptual design aircraft carrier model as the research object, CFD numerical simulations of the ship airwake within the glide path region are carried out using the Poly-Hexcore grid and the detached eddy simulation (DES)/the Reynolds-averaged Navier–Stokes (RANS) turbulence models. Then, using the high spatial resolution ship airwake along the glide path obtained from steady RANS computations under different inflow conditions as a sample dataset, the BP neural network prediction models were trained and optimized. Along the ideal glide path within 200 m behind the stern, the correlation coefficients between the predicted results of the BP neural network and the headwind, crosswind, and vertical wind of the testing samples exceeded 0.95, 0.91, and 0.82, respectively. Finally, using the inflow speed and direction with high temporal resolution from the bow direction obtained by the shipborne DWL as input, the BP prediction models can achieve accurate prediction of the 3D ship airwake along the glide path with high spatiotemporal resolution (3 m, 3 Hz).

title: Effect of Number of Blades with 90° Angle on the Performance of Helix Savonius Vertical Wind Turbine
authors: Abi Mufid Octavio, Moh Jufri, Heni Hendaryati, Ali Saifullah, Achmad Fauzan Hery Soegiharto, Baiq Firyal Salsabila Safitri, Muhammad Sasmito
abstract: Wind energy is a promising renewable energy source that requires efficient conversion methods for optimal utilization. One important factor affecting wind turbine performance is the number of blades, which directly affects energy capture and conversion efficiency. This study investigates the effect of different number of blades (2, 3 and 4) with a fixed blade angle of 90° on the performance of the Helix Savonius Vertical Wind Turbine. The objective is to determine which configuration produces the highest efficiency under varying wind speeds. The methodology used was computational simulation using ANSYS Fluent. From the simulation results, compared to the other configurations, the 3-blade turbine showed the best overall performance as it was able to maintain an optimal balance between lift and drag. This turbine has the most efficient configuration for a wide range of wind speed conditions, especially at medium to high speeds. In contrast, the 2-blade turbine was superior at high wind speeds, but showed a significant drop in efficiency at low speeds. On the other hand, the 4-bladed turbine performs better at low speeds but loses efficiency at higher speeds due to increased aerodynamic drag. Considering all these aspects, the 3-blade design is the optimal choice as it offers the best balance between energy conversion and aerodynamic efficiency.

title: Finite Element Analysis (FEA) of Hydrodynamic Wave Impact on Non-Dewatering Geotube Model
authors: Humaira Hasbullah, Muhammad Farid Shaari, Muhammad Aimullah Abdullah, Siti Hajar Kamari, Siti Nor Amira Hamdan, Helmy Mustafa El Bakry, Khairul Nizam Mustafa
abstract: Non-dewatering geotube contains sludge and water. This is an advantage over dewatering type of geotube as it reduces installation time. However, there are lack of research on the hydrodynamic impact on the non-dewatering geotube. Hence, this preliminary research was conducted to investigate the wave hydrodynamic impact on the yield strength of geotube body. Low-Density Polyethylene (LLDPE) sheet with 1.0mm and 1.5mm thickness was applied to form the geotube. Two wave impact forces had been set as input parameter, which were 867N and 1500N, that acted on the top and the frontal area of the geotube. SolidWorks Finite Element Analysis (FEA) had been employed to simulate the Von Misses Stress (VMS), displacement and strain of the geotube body. The results show that maximum VMS was 9.6MPa, below the LLDPE yield strength failure limit which is at 25MPa. The maximum displacement was recorded at 301mm for 1mm thick LLDPE at 1500N wave load. The maximum geotube strain was 0.713 at 867N wave load on 1mm thickness. These preliminary results show that this proposed geotube specification manage to cater the wave impact.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Propeller Wake Flow Reconstruction Using Hybrid Variables
authors: Gulasi A, Lin Wang, Kaizhou Yao
abstract: This paper explores the application of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINN) in reconstructing the wake flow of a propeller. Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations of the KVLCC2 propeller under open-water conditions are conducted to generate flow field data, which serves as the primary training dataset for the PINN model. A mixed-variable formulation is introduced to formulate and solve the Navier-Stokes (N-S) equations. In traditional Navier-Stokes solvers, high-order derivatives introduce significant computational complexity. By incorporating the Cauchy stress tensor, the reliance on these higher-order derivatives is reduced, which improves the stability of automatic differentiation. The effectiveness of this approach in wake flow reconstruction is evaluated. The results show that the mixed-variable PINN improves the convergence rate and demonstrates strong applicability to propeller flow field prediction and reconstruction.

title: Developing a Digital Twin System Based on a Physics-informed Neural Network for Pipeline Leakage Detection
authors: Wei-Shiang Lin, Yi-Hsiang Cheng, Zhen-Yu Hung, Yuan Yao
abstract: As the demand for resources continues to grow, pipelines have become critical for transporting water, fossil fuels, and chemicals. Monitoring pipeline systems is essential, as leaks can lead to severe environmental damage and safety hazards. This study aims to develop a pipeline leakage detection system based on digital twin technology and Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs). By embedding physical principles, such as the continuity and momentum equations derived from the Navier-Stokes equation, into the neural network's loss function, the model can predict pressure and flow dynamics with high accuracy while adhering to physical constraints. PINNs are particularly advantageous as they require minimal data, maintain physical consistency, and provide reliable interpretations, making them well-suited for addressing pipeline safety challenges. The model is designed to simulate fluid dynamics under normal operating conditions, with deviations in prediction errors signaling potential leaks. Statistical analysis of these errors is used to define control limits, establish rejection regions, and create control charts for leak detection. The detection system is further validated using field data to ensure reliability. By combining physical modeling and neural networks, this approach enhances the accuracy and interpretability of leakage detection, laying a solid foundation for an efficient pipeline monitoring solution.

title: Hybrid Boundary Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Solving Navier-Stokes Equations with Complex Boundary
authors: Chuyu Zhou, Tianyu Li, Chenxi Lan, Rongyu Du, Guoguo Xin, Wei Li, Guoqing Wang, Xun Liu, Hangzhou Yang
abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINN) have achieved notable success in solving partial differential equations (PDE), yet solving the Navier-Stokes equations (NSE) with complex boundary conditions remains a challenging task. In this paper, we introduce a novel Hybrid Boundary PINN (HB-PINN) method that combines a pretrained network for efficient initialization with a boundary-constrained mechanism. The HB-PINN method features a primary network focused on inner domain points and a distance metric network that enhances predictions at the boundaries, ensuring accurate solutions for both boundary and interior regions. Comprehensive experiments have been conducted on the NSE under complex boundary conditions, including the 2D cylinder wake flow and the 2D blocked cavity flow with a segmented inlet. The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on these benchmark scenarios, demonstrating significantly improved accuracy over existing PINN-based approaches.

title: Modeling Arterial Blood Flow Using Physics-Informed Neural Networks
authors: L. Machado, Da Silva, F. Uslenghi, J. P. Borthagaray, S. M. I. R. L. Armentano, F. Castillo
abstract: This paper focuses on the development of a computational model based on Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to simulate arterial blood flow dynamics and its interaction with the arterial wall. PINNs combine the principles of deep learning with partial differential equations, such as the Navier-Stokes equations, to provide an accurate representation of biomechanical phenomena in the cardiovascular system. By incorporating boundary conditions, physical constraints, and specific data, this approach allows for robust simulations even in scenarios with incomplete or sparse data. The proposed framework was validated by predicting key hemodynamic parameters—such as pressure and velocity—across arterial networks. Results highlight its potential for rapid predictions post-training, making it a valuable tool for cardiovascular research and clinical applications. Future work will focus on extending the model to more complex physiological scenarios.

title: A Physics-Informed One-Dimensional Convolutional Neural Network for Modeling Flow Dynamics
authors: Huizhen Huang, Aina Wang, Pan Qin, Xi-Ming Sun
abstract: Accurate characterization of flow dynamics around airfoils is essential for aircraft control design and stability analysis. Navier-Stokes equations are standard models for modeling flow fields around airfoils. Existing physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have demonstrated potential in aircraft control and prediction applications. However, PINNs barely perform well in fields with continuous dynamics and high-frequency characteristics. To enhance aircraft control and design, a physics-informed one-dimensional convolutional neural network (PICNN) is proposed for modeling flow dynamics around airfoils. First, two neural networks are constructed, in which $N e t_{1}$ and $N e t_{2}$ are used to learn temporal dependence and flow dynamics, respectively. Then, $N e t_{1}$ embedding with $N e t_{2}$ is integrated into the data-physics-hybrid loss function. Accordingly, an adaptive training strategy is proposed to mine features and optimize the neural networks. Finally, the effectiveness of PICNN in modeling flow dynamics around airfoils is validated through extensive experiments and comparisons with state-of-the-art methods. Our method can potentially contribute to future advancements in optimizing aircraft control strategies.

title: A sparse and decoupled supervision framework for multi-field reconstruction in turbulent flows using physics-informed neural networks
authors: D. Gomes, António Espírito Santo, J. Páscoa
abstract: Turbulence remains a complex aspect of fluid mechanics, where accurately reconstructing high-resolution turbulent flow fields is particularly challenging due to frequently sparse and incomplete experimental data. This study leverages Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to reconstruct turbulent flow fields from non-uniform, discrete supervisory data, improving adaptability across varying flow conditions and reducing reliance on direct Reynolds stress measurements—often unavailable in practice. Our novel approach enables indirect learning of Reynolds stress tensors from collected velocity and pressure data, streamlining the computational process bypassing complex turbulence model equations. Specifically, the framework excels in navigating complex dynamics within critical engineering structures, such as T-junctions and sudden expansions, typical in water distribution networks. Extensive testing across data scenarios—with sparsity levels of 100%, 10%, 5%, and 2%—demonstrates the model's robustness, maintaining high accuracy in flow prediction and turbulence characterization. The model notably attained L2 errors of 0.0413 and 0.0434 for pressure and velocity reconstruction in critical areas of the 5% dataset. For a sparsity of 2%, the L2 errors were 0.0837 and 0.0769. These results highlight the potential of our PINN framework to complement traditional turbulence modeling approaches by enabling real-time flow monitoring and predictive analytics within digital twin systems, particularly in scenarios where reduced computational cost is essential and full-resolution Reynolds-Averaged Navier–Stokes or Direct Numerical Simulations are impractical.

title: Flow-Rate-Constrained Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Flow Field Error Correction in 4-D Flow Magnetic Resonance Imaging
authors: Jihun Kang, Eui Cheol Jung, H. Koo, D. Yang, Hojin Ha
abstract: In this study, we present enhanced physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), which were designed to address flow field errors in four-dimensional flow magnetic resonance imaging (4D Flow MRI). Flow field errors, typically occurring in high-velocity regions, lead to inaccuracies in velocity fields and flow rate underestimation. We proposed incorporating flow rate constraints to ensure physical consistency across cross-sections. The proposed framework included optimization strategies to improve convergence, stability, and accuracy. Artificial viscosity modeling, projecting conflicting gradients (PCGrad), and Euclidean norm scaling were applied to balance loss functions during training. The performance was validated using 2D computational fluid dynamics (CFD) with synthetic error, in-vitro 4D flow MRI mimicking aortic valve, and in-vivo 4D flow MRI from patients with aortic regurgitation and aortic stenosis. This study demonstrated considerable improvements in correcting flow field errors, denoising, and super-resolution. Notably, the proposed PINNs provided accurate flow rate reconstruction in stenotic and high-velocity regions. This approach extends the applicability of 4D flow MRI by providing reliable hemodynamics in the post-processing stage.

title: Quantifying data needs in surrogate modeling for flow fields in 2D stirred tanks with physics-informed neural networks (PINNs)
authors: Veronika Tr'avn'ikov'a, E. Lieres, Marek Behr
abstract: Stirred tanks are vital in chemical and biotechnological processes, particularly as bioreactors. Although computational fluid dynamics (CFD) is widely used to model the flow in stirred tanks, its high computational cost$-$especially in multi-query scenarios for process design and optimization$-$drives the need for efficient data-driven surrogate models. However, acquiring sufficiently large datasets can be costly. Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) offer a promising solution to reduce data requirements while maintaining accuracy by embedding underlying physics into neural network (NN) training. This study quantifies the data requirements of vanilla PINNs for developing surrogate models of a flow field in a 2D stirred tank. We compare these requirements with classical supervised neural networks and boundary-informed neural networks (BINNs). Our findings demonstrate that surrogate models can achieve prediction errors around 3% across Reynolds numbers from 50 to 5000 using as few as six datapoints. Moreover, employing an approximation of the velocity profile in place of real data labels leads to prediction errors of around 2.5%. These results indicate that even with limited or approximate datasets, PINNs can be effectively trained to deliver high accuracy comparable to high-fidelity data.

title: Modeling Low-Density Polyethylene Polymeryzation in a Multizone-Autoclave Using Coupled Computational Fluid Dynamics and Monte-Carlo Simulation
authors: E. Schwarz, Christoph Weigel, Markus Busch
abstract: The industrial production of low-density polyethylene (LDPE) takes place at temperatures of up to 300 °C and pressures of up to 3000 bar. The properties of the polymer are closely linked to the reaction conditions through the reaction rates of the exothermic radical polymerization, which are determined by pressure, temperature and the concentrations of the species in the system which are influenced by the mixing behavior. To model the complex interplay of these factors, Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations are a powerful tool. CFD allows for the calculation of flow fields by solving the conservation equations for mass, momentum and energy while incorporating the polymerization kinetics for the LDPE polymerization. Based on the converged CFD simulation, it is possible to divide the reactor into several compartments [1], which are assumed to be perfectly mixed continuously stirred tank reactors (CSTR). With a subsequent Monte-Carlo simulation it is possible to determine the molecular weight distribution (MWD) of the polymer. Building on previous work that focused on modeling continuous polymerization in a 100 mL mini-plant autoclave [2,3], this work focuses on modeling a 300 mL multi-zone autoclave that has recently been commissioned [4]. Based on the experimental conditions, a CFD simulation is carried out to compare the simulated results with the experimental data obtained from the size exclusion chromatography (SEC).

title: Research on the cavitation characteristics of axial piston pump based on computational fluid dynamics models
authors: Shaogan Ye, Chengming Zeng, Hesheng Tang, Yue Bao, Jialun Wang, Shendan Zhao, Yan Ren
abstract: Axial piston pumps typically operate under high-pressure and high-speed conditions, making them prone to cavitation, particularly in the piston chambers and grooves of the valve plate. When cavitation bubbles are carried into the high-pressure region by the oil flow, their collapse generates intense pressure pulsations and fluid excitation forces, which significantly affect the working stability of the pump. To study the cavitation characteristics of the axial piston pump, a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) model is developed in this paper, taking into account key flow characteristics. Meanwhile, a cavitation visualization test rig is presented to validate the effectiveness of the simulation model. By investigating the gas-phase volume fraction and jet flow velocity at different locations within the pump, generation mechanisms and dynamic characteristics of cavitation in the piston chambers, grooves, and slippers under various operating conditions are systematically revealed under various operating conditions. Moreover, comparative studies are conducted to examine the evolution of cavitation behavior under different pressures and rotational speeds. The findings elucidate the significant influences of these parameters on cavitation dynamics in axial piston pumps. This work provides a theoretical basis for developing effective strategies to suppress cavitation and enhance pump reliability.
