title: Numerical Study on the Effects of Injection Parameters on Bubble Formation at a Quasi-Static Condition
authors: M. Ibrahim, D. Law
abstract:
The study of bubble formation in liquids is of great importance across various industries and it has many applications including effective heat exchangers, nuclear and bio reactors, and water treatment equipment. An accurate prediction and understanding of bubble formation dynamics are essential in designing and scaling up fluids engineering equipment. Many parameters influence the formation and dynamics of bubble including orifice diameter, surface tension, liquid properties, and volumetric gas flow rate. The present study investigates the formation and detachment dynamics of an isolated air bubble that emerges from an orifice located in the bottom wall and submerged in water under isothermal conditions. Experiments offers the best reliable results comparing with other approaches like numerical and theoretical results, but it is accompanied by the high cost of the experiment and equipment to do the experiment alongside the time and work required to get the results. Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) offers an effective alternative to analyze the effects of the diameter and injection flow rate on bubble characteristics. Numerical analysis schemes based on the Volume of Fluid (VOF) method will be employed. The VOF method is a widely used computational technique for modeling the interface between two fluids, offering an accurate prediction tool for the curvature of the interface between the bubble and the liquid water, and subsequently, the shape of the bubble. The VOF method is based on a transport equation containing a volume fraction variable that is being solved alongside the continuity and momentum equations throughout the computational domain. The conservation equations for mass and momentum were solved and simulated using the open-source software, OpenFOAM, and its packaged solvers. To couple the mass and momentum equations effectively, the PIMPLE algorithm was utilized. The computational domain was represented using a full three-dimensional model. To enhance computational efficiency, a structured mesh was employed, with varying cell sizes throughout the domain.
 The diameter of the orifice and inlet volumetric gas flow rate in the simulation were chosen based on previous experimental studies for validation. The simulation results demonstrated a good agreement with the experimental data available in the literature. This ascertains the importance of the three-dimensional approach in accurately modeling the dynamics of bubble formation and detachment. Additionally, the research analyzed the influence of several key parameters on bubble dynamics, including orifice diameter, contact angle with the bottom wall, and inlet volumetric gas flow rate. Overall, the findings of this research contribute valuable insights into the complexities of bubble dynamics, with implications for various applications in fluid mechanics and engineering.

title: A Review of Machine Learning Application in Aircraft Aerodynamics Development: Methods, Challenges and Prospects
authors: Ziqi Li
abstract: Recently, with the development of AI technology and increasing requirement of Fluid Mechanics analysis, the utilization of machine learning (ML) in engineering is becoming an increasingly prominent research topic. In these engineering subjects, Fluid Mechanics is the one of the most discussed. Some researchers have already done diverse investigations in fusion of ML with Fluid Mechanics calculations. However, due to various limiting factors, the problems of relatively low accuracy, high price and large need of training data still exist in ML prediction results compared to Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) calculations. In this review, the present research in Fluid Mechanics and ML is presented. Achievements of ML application in aircraft optimization are briefly introduced with several examples. Then 3 well-developed ML aircraft aerodynamic performance analysis and optimization methods, which are neural networks (NNs), regression and clustering, are introduced. Their basic concepts and methodologies are analyzed with examples of applications. The benefits and drawbacks are compared, revealing that ML is tend to become another powerful tool of aerodynamics calculations in the near future. The review will provide the readers with a clearer understanding of ML development in aerodynamics.

title: Modeling Partially Observed Nonlinear Dynamical Systems and Efficient Data Assimilation via Discrete-Time Conditional Gaussian Koopman Network
authors: Chuanqi Chen, Zhongrui Wang, Nan Chen, Jin-long Wu
abstract: A discrete-time conditional Gaussian Koopman network (CGKN) is developed in this work to learn surrogate models that can perform efficient state forecast and data assimilation (DA) for high-dimensional complex dynamical systems, e.g., systems governed by nonlinear partial differential equations (PDEs). Focusing on nonlinear partially observed systems that are common in many engineering and earth science applications, this work exploits Koopman embedding to discover a proper latent representation of the unobserved system states, such that the dynamics of the latent states are conditional linear, i.e., linear with the given observed system states. The modeled system of the observed and latent states then becomes a conditional Gaussian system, for which the posterior distribution of the latent states is Gaussian and can be efficiently evaluated via analytical formulae. The analytical formulae of DA facilitate the incorporation of DA performance into the learning process of the modeled system, which leads to a framework that unifies scientific machine learning (SciML) and data assimilation. The performance of discrete-time CGKN is demonstrated on several canonical problems governed by nonlinear PDEs with intermittency and turbulent features, including the viscous Burgers'equation, the Kuramoto-Sivashinsky equation, and the 2-D Navier-Stokes equations, with which we show that the discrete-time CGKN framework achieves comparable performance as the state-of-the-art SciML methods in state forecast and provides efficient and accurate DA results. The discrete-time CGKN framework also serves as an example to illustrate unifying the development of SciML models and their other outer-loop applications such as design optimization, inverse problems, and optimal control.

title: Roadmap to Education 5.0: The Lab Case for Heat and Mass Transfer
authors: D. Galatro, Alessia Di Nardo, Sourojeet Chakraborty
abstract: Education 5.0 (E.D. 5.0) is the education framework developed to align with Industry 5.0 (I.D. 5.0) and Society 5.0 requirements. E.D. 5.0 is human-centric, relying on personalized, collaborative learning. An attribute of E.D. 5.0is the extensive utilization of advanced technology, while promoting critical thinking, ethical considerations, and problem-solving skills. This, rapidly evolving paradigm requires the commitment of Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) to transform curricula, mapping E.D. 5.0 to I.D. 5.0 requirements. This work presents a successful design and implementation of a teaching-learning initiative conducted in the Department of Chemical Engineering & Applied Chemistry of our university, implementing virtual “laboratory-like” sessions for the undergraduate Heat and Mass Transfer course. The purpose of this work is to provide students with enhanced active, experiential and inquiry-based learning, promoting interactive learning through simulations placed across three domains: Data-driven Machine Learning (DD/ML), Process Simulations (PS), and Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), supported through use of chatbots as learning enhancers.
